ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for ginger ale; A0EJX (Carbonated soft drinks) is the closest defensible parent category.

main_ingredients: high fructose corn syrup or sugar

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 330 ml can of Schweppes Ginger Ale, as commonly served in Singapore and food delivery contexts.

nutrient_source.title: Schweppes Ginger Ale — USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods (FDC ID 1104647)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Schweppes Ginger Ale branded product entry in USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods database. Values are per 100 g (approximately 100 ml for this beverage). Protein and fat are confirmed zero by product formulation; energy and carbohydrate are from added sugars.

gi_source.title: Soft drink, carbonated, with sugar — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Schweppes Ginger Ale in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 51 is derived from the closest analogous entry for sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drinks in the University of Sydney GI Database (Fanta Orange GI=68, cola GI=63, average carbonated sugar-sweetened soft drink GI range 58–68). Ginger ale has a lower sugar concentration (~8.8 g/100 g) compared to cola (~10.6 g/100 g), and published peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) reports GI values for carbonated sugar-sweetened beverages in the range of 51–68. A conservative estimate of GI=51 is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (51 × 29.0) / 100 = 14.8; however using 330 ml serving with 8.8 g carb per 100 ml = 29.0 g carb per serving: GL = (51 × 29.0) / 100 = 14.8. Note: GL per serving corrected to 14.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (7 mg per 100 ml)
hypertension.guidance: Schweppes Ginger Ale contains only 7 mg sodium per 100 ml, making it a low-sodium beverage; however, individuals with hypertension should still moderate intake due to added sugar content and its contribution to overall caloric load and potential weight gain, which is a secondary risk factor for elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (8.8 g per 100 ml, entirely from added sugars)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Schweppes Ginger Ale contains 8.8 g of free sugars per 100 ml (approximately 29 g per 330 ml can), which causes a rapid postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit consumption, and a diet version or water should be substituted where possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar intake linked to dyslipidaemia and increased cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages such as Schweppes Ginger Ale is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular disease risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional consumption and prefer unsweetened alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Schweppes Ginger Ale is a globally recognised carbonated soft drink produced by the Schweppes brand, originally founded in the United Kingdom in 1783. It is a clear, lightly golden, effervescent beverage flavoured with natural and artificial ginger flavouring, citric acid, and sweetened with high fructose corn syrup or sugar depending on the market formulation. The drink has a mild, slightly spicy ginger taste with a crisp carbonated finish and a sweet, refreshing character. It is sold in cans and bottles and is widely available in Singapore, cafes, and food delivery platforms. Per 100 ml, it provides approximately 34 kcal of energy, 8.8 g of carbohydrate (entirely from added sugars), negligible protein, and negligible fat. Sodium content is low at approximately 7 mg per 100 ml. It contains no dietary fibre, no significant vitamins or minerals, and is nutritionally classified as an ultra-processed sugar-sweetened beverage. Clinically, it is of concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high free sugar content and moderate glycemic index (~51), which can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation. For cardiovascular health, regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk. It is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and those following halal or gluten-free diets. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a mixer with spirits or as a standalone soft drink accompaniment to meals.
